presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: I'm wheezing and can't catch my breath
My asthma is flaring up badly today
My chest is tight and I'm struggling to breathe
My inhaler isn't working and my breathing is getting worse
I've been wheezing all day and it won't settle

history_data.hpi: Patient with known asthma presents with worsening shortness of breath and audible wheeze over the past 12 hours. Symptoms began after exposure to dust at work and have not improved despite repeated use of salbutamol inhaler. Reports associated chest tightness and dry cough. Denies fever or purulent sputum. No previous ICU admission but had one hospital admission 2 years ago for a severe exacerbation.
patient_quotes: I feel like I can't get enough air in.
My chest feels really tight — it's hard to breathe out.
I've used my inhaler four times today and it's barely helping.
I started wheezing after cleaning out the storage room at work.
I'm scared — this feels worse than usual.
pmh_pool: Asthma — diagnosed age 14, previously well controlled
Allergic rhinitis — ongoing, managed with antihistamines
Previous asthma hospitalisation — 2 years ago, discharged after nebulisers and steroids
No previous ICU admission or intubation

social_history.exercise: Normally active but limiting exercise due to current symptoms

core_facts.inhaler_response: partial — minimal improvement with repeated salbutamol use

negative_history: No leg swelling or calf pain
No previous intubation or ICU admission for asthma

general_appearance: Speaking in short sentences — unable to complete full sentences comfortably
respiratory: Bilateral expiratory wheeze — diffuse throughout lung fields
Accessory muscle use — sternocleidomastoid and intercostal recession

investigations.Peak Flow: Peak expiratory flow rate 42% of predicted best — severe reduction. Confirms significant airways obstruction. Serial peak flows required to monitor response to treatment.
investigations.ABG (Arterial Blood Gas): pH 7.46, PaO2 72 mmHg (hypoxaemia), PaCO2 32 mmHg (hypocapnia — hyperventilation), HCO3 22 mmol/L, SpO2 91% on air. Pattern consistent with moderate to severe asthma. Rising PaCO2 toward normal range would indicate impending respiratory failure.
investigations.Chest X-Ray: Bilateral hyperinflation. No pneumothorax. No consolidation or pleural effusion. No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality beyond hyperinflation. Imaging useful to exclude pneumothorax and pneumonia as precipitants.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 10.8 × 10⁹/L, Eosinophils 0.9 × 10⁹/L (elevated — consistent with atopic/allergic asthma phenotype), Hb 13.6 g/dL, Platelets 274 × 10⁹/L.
investigations.CRP: 18 mg/L — mildly elevated, non-specific. No strong evidence of bacterial infection as precipitant.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 138 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L (mildly low — monitor with repeated salbutamol nebulisations, which can cause hypokalaemia), Creatinine 82 µmol/L, Urea 5.1 mmol/L — otherwise normal.
investigations.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 118 bpm. No acute ST changes. No right heart strain pattern. Tachycardia consistent with hypoxia and salbutamol use.

hidden_diagnosis: Acute Asthma Exacerbation — Moderate to Severe

patient_rules.emotional_state: anxious and frightened — breathing difficulty is distressing

essential_questions: onset and duration of current symptoms
nature of breathlessness — at rest or exertional?
trigger identification — allergen, dust, exercise, URTI, cold air
inhaler use — how many times used today? Any response?
known asthma diagnosis — when diagnosed, usual control
nocturnal symptoms — waking at night with wheeze or cough
allergic history — rhinitis, eczema, food allergy
essential_examinations: general assessment — can patient complete sentences? Using accessory muscles?
auscultation — wheeze, air entry, prolonged expiratory phase
heart rate — tachycardia from hypoxia or salbutamol
peak expiratory flow rate — compare to personal best or predicted
critical_actions: assess severity — mild, moderate, severe, or life-threatening (BTS/SIGN criteria: PEF, SpO2, heart rate, respiratory rate, speech)
identify life-threatening features: silent chest, SpO2 <92%, PaCO2 normal or elevated, poor respiratory effort, altered consciousness
administer salbutamol 2.5–5mg via nebuliser — back-to-back if severe
administer ipratropium bromide 0.5mg nebuliser in moderate-severe cases
administer systemic corticosteroids: prednisolone 40–50mg oral or hydrocortisone 100mg IV if unable to swallow
obtain peak flow before and after treatment to assess response
obtain ABG if SpO2 <92% or signs of impending respiratory failure
consider IV magnesium sulphate 1.2–2g over 20 minutes in severe or life-threatening exacerbation not responding to initial treatment
monitor potassium — repeated salbutamol nebulisations can cause hypokalaemia
escalate to ICU/HDU if deteriorating or life-threatening features present
identify and treat precipitant — infection, allergen, NSAID/aspirin use
red_flags: Silent chest — absence of wheeze with severe distress
Rising PaCO2 on ABG — indicates impending respiratory failure
Previous near-fatal asthma or ICU admission
Failure to respond to initial bronchodilator therapy
acceptable_differentials: COPD exacerbation — but patient is young, non-smoker, known asthmatic
Anaphylaxis — consider if acute allergen exposure, urticaria, or angioedema present
Pulmonary embolism — no leg swelling, low clinical probability
Pneumonia — no fever, consolidation, or purulent sputum
Vocal cord dysfunction — can mimic wheeze, inspiratory stridor, no response to bronchodilators
Acute pulmonary oedema — cardiac wheeze, but no cardiac history, no crackles
management_actions: Oxygen: titrate to SpO2 94–98% via nasal cannulae or face mask
Salbutamol 5mg nebulised immediately — repeat every 15–20 minutes if severe
Ipratropium bromide 0.5mg nebulised — add in moderate-to-severe attacks
Prednisolone 40–50mg oral once daily for 5 days
Magnesium sulphate 1.2–2g IV over 20 minutes — if inadequate response to above
Serial peak flow measurements to track response
Chest X-Ray to exclude pneumothorax or pneumonia
ABG if SpO2 <92% or clinical deterioration
HDU/ICU referral if life-threatening features or failure to respond
Review and optimise preventer therapy before discharge
Written asthma action plan at discharge
Educate on trigger avoidance — occupational dust exposure in this case
critical_history_questions: When exactly did the breathing difficulty start?
Did anything trigger this episode — dust, animals, exercise, cold air, recent cold?
How many times have you used your reliever inhaler today? Did it help?
Are you normally well controlled on your preventer inhaler?
Have you ever been admitted to hospital with your asthma before?
Have you ever been in ICU or needed a breathing tube for your asthma?
Do you wake at night with wheeze or cough?
Do you have allergies — hay fever, eczema, food allergy?
Any recent cold or chest infection before this started?
Any exposure to new chemicals, dust, or fumes at work or home?
Have you taken any aspirin, ibuprofen, or beta-blockers recently?
Do you have a personal best peak flow reading?
Any chest pain, leg swelling, or recent travel?
